entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
The conversation between Erik Aronesty and Anthony Towns delves into the implications of a sustained 51% attack on Bitcoin's network, particularly focusing on its effects on mining difficulty and economic incentives. A major concern highlighted is that during such an attack, if all non-censoring participants cease their mining activities, the difficulty of mining could potentially decrease to its minimum level. This reduction in difficulty lowers the cost of mining, making it feasible for more participants, including those with less powerful computing resources like CPUs, to join the mining efforts again.
This scenario assumes that transaction fees would increase as a result of unconfirmed transactions accumulating, thereby creating a financial incentive for miners to counteract the censorship. The censor, in order to maintain control, would need to continue mining at a loss—subsidizing the blockchain to prevent competitors from profiting from higher fee transactions. This situation illustrates a complex trade-off between maintaining network security through mining incentives and the potential for reduced censorship resistance as difficulty decreases.
The discussion also touches upon the broader implications of substituting inflation-based block rewards for fee-based ones, questioning the trade-off between censorship resistance and security against double-spending. It is noted that conventional banking systems typically provide reliable security against double spends. The dialogue suggests that Satoshi Nakamoto might not have fully anticipated these dynamics when designing Bitcoin’s economic model, particularly regarding the phase-out of block subsidies. The cessation of these subsidies is seen as a critical transition point for Bitcoin, moving from a potentially censorable to a more robustly censorship-resistant currency.
Ultimately, the conversation underscores the belief that it is economic forces—rather than purely technological solutions—that ensure the security and viability of cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin. This perspective highlights the importance of demand for such currencies in supporting a model of censorship resistance, without which the underlying economic and security models fail to hold up.
